Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic. I work part-time to cover my expenses, so I'd squeeze in study sessions whenever I could - sometimes early mornings before work, sometimes late at night. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd try to do practice problems or work through examples physically rather than just reading. With 20 courses to juggle, I couldn't really stick to a perfect schedule, but I tried to dedicate at least a few hours most days when I wasn't exhausted.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what shifts I had at work. When deadlines were approaching, I'd cram more hours in, but during quieter weeks I might slack off a bit because I was just tired. I tried to set regular times on weekends, but life kept getting in the way. Being 25 and managing everything on my own means flexibility isn't always a choice - it's a necessity.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or that nagging anxiety in the back of my head telling me I was falling behind. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were new announcements or if I'd missed something important. The anxiety was real - even when I felt bored with the content, I'd still feel this pressure to at least see what was happening. Occasionally I'd log in hoping to find something that would actually grab my interest.
Q4: There were a few weeks where I got into a groove of checking the LMS every morning with my coffee before heading out. It became almost automatic, like checking messages. But then work schedules would shift or I'd have a rough week financially, and that routine would fall apart. I'd have to rebuild the habit from scratch, which got frustrating. The mindfulness exercises they suggested actually helped me get back into routines when I lost them.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I could pause, rewind, and actually work through problems alongside them - that hands-on approach works best for my brain. I watched a lot of video content, over three hours worth that I found genuinely helpful. Discussion forums were okay when people shared practical examples or real-world applications. Anything where I could actively do something rather than passively read was valuable to me.
Q6: I often skipped the long reading materials and theoretical PDFs - they just made me zone out completely. Some of the discussion prompts felt pointless, like busy work that wouldn't help me actually understand anything. When you're bored and anxious at the same time, you start prioritizing ruthlessly. I'd ignore anything that felt like it was just padding the course rather than teaching me something practical.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to something I'd need to know or do - whether for exams or real life. If I could physically work through a problem or apply a concept, I was engaged. When something was purely theoretical with no clear purpose, I'd lose interest fast. I also valued activities that didn't take forever because my time is genuinely limited with work and everything else.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. Required ones got done because they had to, even if I was running on empty. With optional stuff, I'd think 'that looks interesting' but then reality would hit and I'd prioritize survival mode. The few optional things I did engage with were ones that seemed like they'd directly help my exam scores, not just enrichment for enrichment's sake.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download videos and PDFs to study on my phone during bus rides to work or during slow periods at my job. I couldn't always have internet access or sit at a computer, so offline studying was essential. I also used YouTube videos and free resources online when the course materials weren't clicking for me. My notes were all handwritten because writing things out helps me remember better than typing.
Q10: Most of my real learning happened away from the computer. I'd take concepts from the videos and work through them on paper, sometimes spending hours on practice problems that never showed up in any log. During work breaks, I'd quiz myself mentally on material I'd reviewed earlier. The LMS would just show I downloaded something once, but that file might have been my main study resource for weeks.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video or a problem set once, then work through it multiple times offline over the following days. My internet at home isn't always reliable, and I can't afford to use data streaming videos repeatedly. So one download might represent five or six study sessions that happened completely off the grid. It was more practical for my situation.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety spiked and I just couldn't focus on the course content at all. When money got tight or work was demanding, the course felt like this overwhelming thing I couldn't deal with. The boredom didn't help either - some modules were so dry that engaging felt like a chore. During those times, I'd still try to show up, but my brain wasn't really there.
Q13: Massively. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I often had to choose between studying and earning money to eat - not a real choice. The confusion from some poorly explained concepts made me want to give up on certain topics entirely. I'd end up doing the bare minimum on the LMS and hoping I could catch up later.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have videos playing while I was too tired to actually pay attention, or I'd scroll through readings without any of it sticking. My body was present but my mind was somewhere else - worrying about bills or just mentally checked out from exhaustion. Those sessions probably looked like engagement in the data, but they were basically useless. I'd have to redo everything later anyway.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. Part of it was the anxiety making everything feel overwhelming, so I'd avoid starting because starting meant confronting how behind I was. Sometimes I was just too bored with the material to force myself to engage. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would become next week. My resilience usually kicked in eventually, but often too late.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit anything. I'd have this pattern of ignoring work until the pressure became unbearable, then cramming like crazy at the last minute. The LMS would show me being inactive for days, then suddenly very active right before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but with everything else going on in my life, that urgency was what I needed to actually produce work.
Q17: When I got feedback, it could go either way. Positive feedback motivated me to keep trying, but critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse and I'd avoid that topic for a while. The problem was feedback often came too late to actually help me improve for the next thing. When feedback was specific and showed me exactly what to fix, I engaged more. Vague comments just frustrated me.
Q18: The course structure was honestly overwhelming with so many modules and requirements. I needed more flexibility given my work schedule, and the rigid deadlines didn't account for people like me who can't always control their time. The structure assumed everyone could engage consistently, which just wasn't my reality. I appreciated when there were clear priorities so I knew what absolutely had to get done versus what was nice-to-have.
Q19: Having video content was great for my learning style - much easier than walls of text. But the sheer volume of material made it hard to know where to focus my limited time. When modules built on each other clearly, I could follow along better. What made it harder was when expectations were unclear or when there were too many small tasks that felt like busywork. Simpler, more focused design would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think those students might be like me - logging in, clicking around, maybe watching videos, but not having the time, energy, or clarity to actually complete assignments. High activity could mean someone is struggling to understand and keeps revisiting material without making progress. Or they're dealing with life stuff that prevents them from finishing things even though they're trying. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: That actually describes part of my experience. Some of my best studying happened completely offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. Someone could have low LMS activity but be grinding through practice problems on paper every day. The system only sees what happens on the platform, not what happens in someone's notebook or in their head during their commute. Real learning isn't always visible.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone logs in, what they click on, and whether they submit things on time. That's useful for spotting someone who's completely disappeared. But logs miss the quality of engagement - was someone actually learning or just going through motions? They miss offline work entirely, and they can't capture someone's circumstances, anxiety, or genuine effort that doesn't translate to clicks.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to check boxes. It's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, trying to connect concepts to things I already know or will need to know. For me, it's physical too - writing, working through problems, doing something with my hands. True engagement is when learning feels purposeful, even if it's hard. It's not about time spent; it's about mental presence.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied on my phone during work breaks, or the hours I spent with handwritten notes trying to make sense of difficult concepts. It wouldn't capture my anxiety making simple tasks feel impossible, or my resilience in coming back after setbacks. It wouldn't show that I actually cared about learning even when my completion rates looked terrible. My circumstances - working, limited resources, managing everything alone at 25 - none of that shows up in click data.
